---
title: "Selection and Insertion Sorts"
description: "The two simplest sorts --- how they work, and why they still earn their keep."
date: 2026-07-10
categories: [Algorithms, Method]
---

Sorting is one of the most basic approaches to solving problems in human
thought. Be it prioritizing which tasks to tackle first or who to pay first;
ranking in competitions, exams, and any other comparative decisions --- we are
sorting. It is a fundamental problem in computer science too: organizing records
by date, tabulating results into a sequence of some kind. In this post we look at
two of the simplest sort algorithms --- selection sort and insertion sort. They
are instructive, and although less efficient than other algorithms on large,
randomly ordered data, they remain widely used at small sizes and on streaming
data. The goal here is to introduce both and, in a companion post, put one to
work on a real puzzle.

## Selection Sort

The idea: repeatedly *select* the smallest remaining value and place it in the
next position --- smallest into slot 0, next-smallest into slot 1, and so on
until every element is placed.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Pseudocode

```
SELECTION-SORT(a):
    n ← length(a)
    for i ← 0 to n-1:
        min ← i
        for j ← i+1 to n-1:
            if a[j] < a[min]:
                min ← j
        swap a[i] and a[min]      # at most n-1 swaps total
    return a
```

### Python

```{python}
def selection_sort(a):
    n = len(a)
    for i in range(n):
        smallest = i
        for j in range(i + 1, n):
            if a[j] < a[smallest]:
                smallest = j
        a[i], a[smallest] = a[smallest], a[i]   # pythonic swap, no temp needed
    return a

original = [5, 3, 4, 1, 2, 10, 1]
print("input: ", original)
print("sorted:", selection_sort(original.copy()))   # .copy(): it sorts in place
```

:::

The distinctive property: selection sort makes at most **n − 1 swaps**, the
fewest of the elementary sorts. That barely matters in RAM, but it matters a lot
when a *write* is the expensive operation.

## Insertion Sort

The idea is how most people sort a hand of cards. Keep the cards to your left
sorted; pick up the next one; slide it left past every larger card until it
lands in the right spot.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Pseudocode

```
INSERTION-SORT(a):
    n ← length(a)
    for i ← 0 to n-1:
        for j ← i downto 1:
            if a[j] < a[j-1]:
                swap a[j] and a[j-1]
            else:
                break             # already in order from here; stop early
    return a
```

### Python

```{python}
def insertion_sort(a):
    n = len(a)
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(i, 0, -1):             # lazy range: the break actually saves work
            if a[j] < a[j-1]:
                a[j], a[j-1] = a[j-1], a[j]    # swap into place
            else:
                break                          # already in order here; stop early
    return a

original = [5, 3, 4, 1, 2, 10, 1]
print("input: ", original)
print("sorted:", insertion_sort(original.copy()))
```

:::

## How they scale

Claims about running time are cheap; let's measure. The two functions above are
timed below on three kinds of input --- randomly shuffled, already sorted, and
reversed --- across growing sizes, taking the best of a few trials at each size.

```{python}
#| label: fig-runtime
#| fig-cap: "Running time vs. input size. Both panels share a y-scale, so the gaps are real."
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show the benchmark code"
import time, random
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def best_ms(sort, data, trials=3):
    best = float("inf")
    for _ in range(trials):
        d = data.copy()                 # fresh copy: the sorts mutate in place
        t = time.perf_counter()
        sort(d)
        best = min(best, time.perf_counter() - t)
    return best * 1000

sizes = [100, 200, 400, 800, 1600]
cases = {
    "random":   lambda n: random.sample(range(n * 3), n),
    "sorted":   lambda n: list(range(n)),
    "reversed": lambda n: list(range(n, 0, -1)),
}
random.seed(0)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9, 3.6), sharey=True)
for ax, (name, algo) in zip(axes, [("Selection sort", selection_sort),
                                   ("Insertion sort", insertion_sort)]):
    for label, gen in cases.items():
        ax.plot(sizes, [best_ms(algo, gen(n)) for n in sizes],
                marker="o", label=label)
    ax.set_title(name)
    ax.set_xlabel("input size n")
    ax.grid(alpha=0.3)
axes[0].set_ylabel("time (ms)")
axes[1].legend(title="input order")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Two things jump out. **Selection sort's three curves lie almost on top of one
another** --- it doesn't care whether the input is sorted, shuffled, or reversed,
because it always scans the entire unsorted remainder and always makes the same
n − 1 swaps. Input order is invisible to it.

**Insertion sort fans out completely.** On sorted input it is essentially flat:
the `break` fires on the first comparison of every pass, the work is linear, and
at n = 1600 it runs on the order of a hundred times faster than selection sort.
On random input it is quadratic and slightly *worse* than selection (more data
movement). On reversed input --- its worst case, where the `break` never fires
--- it is the slowest line on the whole plot. The same algorithm is both the
fastest and the slowest curve in the figure, decided entirely by the order of
its input. That is what *adaptive* means, made visible.

## Where these still earn their keep

<!-- Expand these in your own voice --- each is a real, checkable claim: -->

- **Insertion sort is *online*.** It can absorb elements one at a time as they
  arrive, keeping the array sorted after each insertion --- so it fits streaming
  input, where a divide-and-conquer sort would have to wait for the whole batch.
- **Insertion sort is *adaptive*.** On nearly-sorted input the inner loop hits
  the `break` almost immediately, so it approaches O(n). This is why real-world
  hybrid sorts (like the Timsort behind Python's own `sorted`) fall back to
  insertion sort on short runs, where its low overhead beats an asymptotically
  faster algorithm.
- **Selection sort minimizes writes.** Its n − 1 swaps make it attractive when
  writing is costly or wearing --- the seed of the companion post's puzzle.

*Next:* an applied post that takes the "fewest exchanges" idea and runs with it
--- sorting a deck of cards laid face down, where every exchange is the costly
move.
